topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a dropped book. Professor Albright was leaving. It was a stark, impersonal announcement, yet the air in my small apartment seemed to thicken instantly, pressing down on my chest. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into meaningless symbols. My hands began to shake, a fine tremor that made gripping my lukewarm coffee cup a clumsy ordeal. Every familiar corner of my study desk suddenly felt accusatory, saturated with the ghost of his presence. I reread the message three times, searching for a loophole, a reprieve, anything to halt the inevitable. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I pushed back from my chair, the scrape of the wood against the floor sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet. I needed to call him, to demand an explanation, but my throat constricted, producing only a dry, rasping sound.

[story 2]

I walked across campus as if wading through thick mud, the afternoon sun doing nothing to warm the chill that had settled deep in my bones. Each familiar pathway, the route I took daily to the library, now felt like a trap. When I finally reached the departmental building, the sight of Professor Albright’s empty office door was a physical blow. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I stopped just outside, leaning against the cool brickwork, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying sense of disorientation. Inside, the silence was oppressive, heavy with unspoken departures and unresolved deadlines. I sank onto a nearby bench, pulling my knees to my chest, focusing intensely on the pattern of cracks in the pavement. A tightness coiled in my stomach, a knot of pure anxiety that refused to loosen, making every shallow breath feel like a deliberate act of endurance.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the student union hummed with an irritating, relentless buzz. I clutched the printout of the faculty announcement so tightly my knuckles were white, the paper crinkling under the pressure. My vision swam slightly at the edges. It wasn't the news itself that was the source of the distress; it was the sudden vacuum where his guidance used to be. I paced the length of the crowded walkway, my steps hurried and uneven, desperate to outrun the feeling of being utterly adrift. People moved around me, oblivious, their conversations a distant, muffled tide. I stopped abruptly by a window, looking out at the bustling quad, feeling intensely isolated in my own internal storm. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to physically block out the reality that this crucial piece of my academic structure was dissolving.
